name: Pillai Lokacharya
dates_notes: Roughly contemporary with Vedānta Deśika; sectarian dating.

lineage_polemical.note: The Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai split formally crystallizes around Pillai-Lokācārya's cat-hold (mārjāra-nyāya) account vs. Vedānta Deśika's monkey-hold (markaṭa-nyāya).

core_thesis: Pillai Lokācārya is the school-defining figure of Tenkalai Śrīvaiṣṇavism, articulating in Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa what Vedānta Deśika contemporaneously articulates in Sanskrit Vaḍakalai. The canonical Viśiṣṭādvaita architecture is preserved — Brahman as Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī, qualified by *cit* and *acit* in *apṛthak-siddha* relation as his body (*Tattva-Trayam*, *Īśvara-prakaraṇa* §§1–35) — but the soteriology is pushed in a specific direction, fixed in *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§270–306 and *Mumukṣuppadi* §§246–278. The Vaḍakalai treats the six *aṅga*-s of Rāmānuja's *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya* as conditions the surrendered self must enact; Pillai-Lokācārya argues that this construal smuggles *bhakti-yoga*-style qualifications back into *prapatti* and so makes grace conditional on the agent's continuing co-action — a position incompatible, on his reading, with the *ākiñcana*-language of Yāmuna's *Stotra-Ratna* v. 22 and *Bhagavad-Gītā* 18.66 (the *carama-śloka*). *Prapatti* (*śaraṇāgita*) is therefore the only operative *upāya* (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§270–306), and its essence is utter, conditionless surrender of the *ākiñcana* to Bhagavān's spontaneous *kṛpā* (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §83). The six *aṅga*-s are descriptions of what surrender already is, not conditions the agent must satisfy (*Mumukṣuppadi* §§271–278). Bhagavān's grace is the sole effective cause of liberation; the *jīva*'s contribution is to recognise its own incapacity (*kārpaṇya*) (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§115–124). The image is the *mārjāra-nyāya* — the kitten that the mother cat carries by the scruff — against the Vaḍakalai *markaṭa-nyāya* of the monkey-baby that grips its mother (the contrast crystallises against Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra*, *prapatti-yoga-adhikāra*). Two further Tenkalai-distinctive consequences follow. Śrī functions as *upāya* in concert with Nārāyaṇa, not merely as *puruṣakāra* (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§9–42, the *puruṣakāra-prakaraṇa*; against Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra*, *puruṣakāra-vaibhava-adhikāra*); and *bhāgavata-śeṣatva* — being-for-the-devotee — exceeds even direct *Bhāgavat-śeṣatva* (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§406–438), so that the lived norm of the school is the *prabandham*-grounded community of the *bhāgavata*-s rather than the individual contemplative.

engaged_works.title: Shri-Vacana-Bhushana
engaged_works.summary: A Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa rahasya in 463 *cūrṇikā*-s — the school-defining text of Tenkalai Śrīvaiṣṇavism. The treatise synthesises the Āḻvārs' Tamil *prabandham* with the doctrinal positions of Nāmmāḻvār, Nāthamuni, Yāmuna, and Rāmānuja into a single articulation of *prapatti*-soteriology, point by point in opposition to the position Deśika is contemporaneously developing in the *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra*. Five load-bearing claims fix the school. First, *prapatti* is the only *upāya*; *bhakti-yoga* is unnecessary and, for the *ākiñcana*, an obstacle, since the agent who undertakes *bhakti-yoga* presupposes the very competence the *ākiñcana* lacks (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§270–306). Second, the operative cause of *mokṣa* is Bhagavān's spontaneous *kṛpā*, not any quality or action of the surrendered self; the *jīva* contributes recognition of incapacity (*kārpaṇya*), nothing else (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§83, 115–124). Third, Śrī is *upāya* alongside Nārāyaṇa, not merely *puruṣakāra* (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§9–42); Yāmuna's *Catuḥ-Ślokī* is read as making her grace itself the operative principle. Fourth, *bhāgavata-śeṣatva* — being-for-the-devotee — exceeds direct *Bhāgavat-śeṣatva* as the highest ontological status (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§406–438), since service to the Lord through the devotee is more conformable to the Lord's own preference than service rendered directly. Fifth, sin (*pāpa*) committed *after* surrender is not removed by penance but is itself an occasion for the Lord's forgiving love (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§356–393, the *aparādha-prakaraṇa*) — a claim widely contested by the Vaḍakalai as licence and defended by the Tenkalai as the necessary consequence of conditionless grace. *Ācārya-niṣṭhā* is fixed at §§439–450. The work is the canonical Tenkalai counterweight to the *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* — articulating in lived Maṇipravāḷa idiom a soteriology in which agency belongs entirely to grace and the surrendered self's only act is recognition of incapacity.

engaged_works.title: Mumukshuppadi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored exposition of the three rahasyas (tirumantra, dvaya, caramaśloka).
engaged_works.summary: A Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa rahasya in 278 *cūrṇikā*-s expounding the three Śrīvaiṣṇava esoteric *mantra*-s — the *tirumantra* (*aum namo nārāyaṇāya* / *aṣṭākṣara*), the *dvaya-mantra* (the two-clause surrender-formula), and the *carama-śloka* (*Bhagavad-Gītā* 18.66). The exposition unfolds the soteriological logic of *prapatti* from the inner grammar of each *mantra*. The *tirumantra* discloses the surrendering self's *śeṣatva* — its being-for-Bhagavān as ontologically constitutive, not as added vocation. The *dvaya-mantra* enacts surrender to the joint refuge of Nārāyaṇa and Śrī, with the two clauses fixing first the act of taking refuge and then the prayer for service. The *carama-śloka* (*sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja, ahaṃ tvā sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ*) supplies the divine assurance of liberation through that surrender alone. The reading insists, against the Vaḍakalai, that *prapatti* is constitutively conditionless and that its supposed *aṅga*-s are descriptions of the surrendered state and not pre-conditions to be enacted: the *carama-śloka*'s *parityajya* ("having renounced") is taken to require renunciation of the *aṅga*-conditions themselves as instruments. The work is the most widely recited Tenkalai rahasya, used in daily catechetical instruction; Maṇavāḷa Māmunigaḷ's commentary on it became the school's canonical theological textbook. It is the locus where the *mārjāra-nyāya* theology of grace receives its most sustained mantric grounding.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Trayam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored exposition of the three categories (cit, acit, Īśvara).
engaged_works.summary: A Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa rahasya systematically expounding the three ontological categories of Viśiṣṭādvaita — *cit* (conscious selves), *acit* (non-conscious matter, comprising *prakṛti*, *kāla*, and the *śuddha-sattva* of the eternal realm), and *Īśvara* (Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī) — and the relations among them. Where Vedānta Deśika treats the same material in technical Sanskrit (*Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa*, *Nyāya-Siddhāñjana*), Pillai-Lokācārya gives the doctrine in the Maṇipravāḷa idiom of the temple-community. *Cit* is the eternal *jīva* whose essence is *jñātṛtva* (knower-status) and whose ontological status is *śeṣa* — subordinate-belonging — to Bhagavān; the *śeṣa-śeṣin* relation is constitutive, not consequent on action. *Acit* in its three modes constitutes the field of *jīva*-experience and the body of Bhagavān; *prakṛti*'s instability and *kāla*'s pervasiveness are functions Bhagavān exercises through them. *Īśvara* is the unique independent reality whose body is the totality of *cit* and *acit* and whose own essence is *ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa* — infinite auspicious attributes — together with Śrī. The Maṇipravāḷa register matters: the same ontology that Deśika argues against Sāṃkhya, Nyāya, and Buddhism is here exhibited as the implicit metaphysics of the *prabandham*-community's worship, available without Sanskritic technical apparatus. The work pairs with the *Mumukṣuppadi* on rahasya-meaning and the *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* on soteriology to form the doctrinal triad on which Maṇavāḷa Māmunigaḷ later commented.

engaged_works.title: Artha-Panchaka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored exposition of the five-fold meaning.
engaged_works.summary: A short Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa rahasya organising the entire Śrīvaiṣṇava soteriological programme under five headings — the *artha-pañcaka*. First, *para-svarūpa*: the nature of the supreme (Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī as Brahman *saviśeṣa*, qualified by infinite auspicious attributes). Second, *sva-svarūpa*: the nature of the *jīva* (eternal, atomic, *śeṣa* to Bhagavān). Third, *upāya-svarūpa*: the nature of the means (on the Tenkalai reading, *prapatti* alone, conditionless). Fourth, *puruṣārtha-svarūpa*: the nature of the goal (eternal *kaiṅkarya* in the *parama-pada*). Fifth, *virodhi-svarūpa*: the nature of the obstacles (the *jīva*'s misidentifications and false attachments — the misreading of *śeṣatva* as autonomy, of *upāya* as effort, of *puruṣārtha* as enjoyment). The five-fold scheme functions as the basic catechetical map by which a Tenkalai *bhāgavata* is instructed in the school's complete teaching. The work is the most compact and pedagogically central of the *aṣṭādaśa-rahasya* — the eighteen secret tracts attributed to Pillai-Lokācārya — and the *artha-pañcaka* structure remains the curriculum of Tenkalai catechesis to the present.
